content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. Consider the sequence containing the integers 1,2,…,N1, 2, \dots, N1,2,…,N in increasing order (each exactly once). Find the length of the longest subarray in this sequence such that the bitwise AND of all elements in the subarray is **positive**.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single integer NNN on a single line.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the length of the longest subarray that satisfy the given property.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the maximum possible length of a contiguous subarray of [1,2,…,N] whose bitwise AND of all elements is positive.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only subarray is [1] (AND=1). For N=2 any subarray longer than 1 contains 1 whose AND becomes 0, so answer 1. For N=3 the subarray [2,3] has AND=2, length 2. For N=4 the best is [2,3] (AND=2) length 2. For N=7 the interval [4,7] has all numbers with the 3rd bit set, AND=4, length 4.

N=5: p=4, len1=2, len2=2, answer 2 (subarray [4,5]). N=8: p=8, len1=1, len2=4, answer 4 (subarray [4,7]). N=10: p=8, len1=3, len2=4, answer 4 (subarray [8,11] truncated to [8,10] gives length 3 but the interval [4,7] of length 4 still has positive AND, so answer 4).

solution.approach: Let p be the greatest power of two not exceeding N. Any subarray whose AND is positive must have at least one bit that is set in every element; the most significant set bit of the subarray determines the longest possible interval. All numbers that have the same most‑significant bit form a contiguous block. The two candidate blocks are: 1) the block that ends at N and starts at p, i.e., [p, N] with length len1 = N‑p+1, and 2) the block just below p, i.e., [p/2, p‑1] with length len2 = p/2. The answer is max(len1, len2). The value p can be obtained in O(1) using the highest set‑bit operation (e.g., 1<<(bit_length‑1) in Python or __builtin_clz in C/C++). The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) overall.
